text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] YUFENG GUO: The General
Purpose Transformer, or GPT, has really transformed
the AI landscape over the last few years. In this workshop, we'll
learn about its origins and internal structure, and
we'll build one from scratch. Hi, my name is Yufeng, and I'm
a developer advocate at Google. Today, we'll check
out the process of building a transformer
on JAX using the Flax NNX library to build the model
architecture, Optax for loss function and optimizer
creation, and train on accelerated hardware with
the help of Orbax and XLA. You'll get a taste of what
development on JAX looks like, and be prepared to
take your next step into the world of building
and training AI models. Now, before we
dive into the code, I want to set the
stage a little bit and give some background
on transformers. If you're already
familiar with this and you want to skip
ahead to the coding, please, by all means, feel free. I certainly won't be offended. When transformers and large
language models were first coming onto the scene,
they were made specifically for processing text. Nowadays, many large
language models are what we call
multi-modal, meaning they can work with other modes
of communication, including pictures, video, and audio. But how does a computer really
know what any words mean? It all comes down
to embeddings, a way to turn human-understandable
concepts into numbers that the computer can
process and interpret. Initially, the entire
vocabulary that a language model is expected to see is placed
sort of in random positions throughout what we might
consider a higher dimensional space. This is called the
embedding space. Now, how many dimensions
are we talking about? It might be hundreds or even
thousands of dimensions. This allows the
embedding to capture a lot of understanding and
nuance about each and every term in its vocabulary. Then through the
training process, involving looking at all of the
text in its training data set, these text embeddings
are updated to reflect
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – MmxPBgrrn4Q
text:
a lot of understanding and
nuance about each and every term in its vocabulary. Then through the
training process, involving looking at all of the
text in its training data set, these text embeddings
are updated to reflect the meaning of the words
and their relationships to one another. For instance, the
word jump might be close to other physical
activities and exercises, and it might also, perhaps
along a different dimension, be close to terms about car
repair and jumping a car. The key takeaway here is that
when machine learning models handle text these
days, they're typically looking at it through the
lens of an embedding space rather than simply
the characters of a word that you and I see. If you want to learn
more about embeddings, there are some great
detailed explanations at the Machine Learning Crash
Course at developers.google.com. And if you want to access some
existing embeddings that already have a large vocabulary trained
and ready for you to use, you can use text embeddings
from Google Cloud. It even has multilingual
embedding models. Now let's turn our attention
to the real reason you're here, transformers. Depending on who you ask, it
all started back in 2017 with the research paper famously
titled "Attention is All You Need," from Google Brain. The concepts presented
in this paper lie at the very heart of large
language models today, and it led to further
research of variations on the original
ideas, many of which are still being developed
and explored to this day. You can read the
paper if you'd like. It's quite well-written. In this video, we'll
go over some highlights so we can better understand
what we'll be doing in the code. The transformer architecture
focuses on a novel operation called attention, hence
the title of the paper, coupling it with a number
of other operations that were commonplace among
machine learning models at the time. Let's dig into this further. The task that the
researchers were able to figure
out with attention was a way to numerically
calculate and describe
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – MmxPBgrrn4Q
text:
that were commonplace among
machine learning models at the time. Let's dig into this further. The task that the
researchers were able to figure
out with attention was a way to numerically
calculate and describe the relationship between
words in the context that they were being used. Moreover, it allowed
the words themselves to be that guide as
to the relationships amongst each other. The inputs to attention
were three vectors called query, key, and
value, typically referred to as Q, K and V. You can think of the query
vector as describing, for each word in a sentence
or phrase, what other words it cares about. What is it looking for? The key vector is
a way to represent what a word wants to present
about itself in response to those other queries. And the value vector encodes
what it kind of offers in the event of a good match. Another way to
describe it is perhaps with a handshake, along
with a gift exchange. The query is someone
reaching out their hand, and the key is the other
hand coming out to meet it. And if they
successfully match up, then the value is what
is then handed over if a handshake is established. There are a couple
of other things we see here in the
attention formula aside from Q, K, and V. First,
there is the softmax function. What is softmax? Softmax normalizes the
values coming out of Q.K, ensuring that they all
sum to 1, and shows us which values are the highest. So if one value is minorly
larger than the others, it might end up
with a value of 0.8, while the rest sum up to 0.2. So it all comes together as 1. If the two biggest values
are both about the same, it's like a tie for first,
they might each have, say, a value of 0.4, while the rest
of the values sum up to 0.2. Second, we see
that Q.K is divided by the square root
of D. D here refers to the embedding dimension
used in our text embedding. So if we had an embedding
dimension of, say, 64, we'll divide the entire
dot product by 8. Why are we doing this? The reason is to scale
down the value that gets passed into softmax.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – MmxPBgrrn4Q

to the embedding dimension
used in our text embedding. So if we had an embedding
dimension of, say, 64, we'll divide the entire
dot product by 8. Why are we doing this? The reason is to scale
down the value that gets passed into softmax. Even though softmax is going to
normalize our values regardless, the way that it
computes this means that the higher the absolute
value of all the terms are, the more those resulting
decimals get pulled to extremes. So instead of having values
that are small and have kind of small differences, it
might select the largest one, even if it's only
larger by a little bit, and just make it way bigger. In other words, it makes the
distribution sharper or peakier. All of this comes
together to make what is called scaled
dot-product attention. The bottom diagram uses
this and scales it up. So when we talk about
multi-headed attention, it's referring to multiple
parallel implementations of attention, all happening
independently from one another. This allows the model to
attend to different aspects of the relationships
in the data. There's clearly a lot
happening here in this diagram, and I cannot hope to explain
it all in this video alone. Otherwise, we'll never
get to the workshop part. A couple of particularly
good resources are out there, which discuss

operation in the transformer with fantastic
animated graphics. Once you've gone through
that and finished this video, you'll be really well set up
to understand chapter four of the free book titled,
"How to Scale Your Model," from DeepMind. That chapter is titled "All

better understand how all this code, numbers, and data work
together with the incredible infrastructure used
to train these models. We'll definitely be sure to put
these links in the resources below. But wait, there is more. The whole transformer
model, well, that's a lot more than just attention alone.

to train these models. We'll definitely be sure to put

to get things started.

have eight TPU v2 chips. Next, we're going to set
up the device mesh, which will allow us to distribute
our data set and our model across these devices that
we have available to us. In this case, we'll
split up our data eight ways across
eight chips, keeping a full copy of the model
on every single chip. This is sometimes referred
to as data parallelism, since the data is being
split up and processed in parallel while the model
remains whole and undivided. There are other
ways to split up, or shard, our data and model. For example, you
might split the data up four ways instead
of eight, and then split the model in half
so that each copy is distributed across two chips. Think of it as a 4
by 2 grid, or mesh, that contains four
pairs of model halves, and then two copies of each
batch of data split four ways. Since we're aiming to
simulate the results of GPT-2, our code uses the embeddings
released with the GPT-2 model. We'll retrieve them using
the tiktoken library. The next cell sets
us up with a number of hyperparameters, which
define how our training run will be set up. These numbers are focused around
how best to utilize the hardware that we're training on. We'll be using the GPT-2
branch of the code, with a batch size of 24
on Colab and 72 on Kaggle. This illustrates the difference,
in part, between TPU v2 and v3. We can achieve a
batch size that's three times larger with TPU v3. Now let's check out the model
structure of the transformer. We'll see how to use the
NNX library, which stands for Neural Networks for JAX. And let's start from the top

class, what do we have here is the
TokenAndPositionEmbedding class. This one is fairly

the position of the words. As we briefly touched on in the

understands what words mean, but also where words are. Notice that each

and has an init method and a call method. The call method is where
we really wire together
